content.body: Shannon Booth sideshowbarker want to review https://github.com/whatwg/dom/pull/1477?
I pushed some nits, but I think this latest iteration needs some more work.
left a comment about one extra deviation we have in place, though I think it is separate
Oops looking at it again it was a bit sloppy in terms of style. sorry about that. Fixing now.
Should be ready for another look. I fixed all the style regressions.
Seems like WATTSI is giving a 503 (https://github.com/whatwg/html/pull/12624), anything to do when that happens?
ok seems to be fixed the 3rd time
Shannon Booth: hello again, I think https://github.com/whatwg/dom/pull/1479 should fix that NodeIterator issue. If you know of a less involved fix I'm all ears.
content.formatted_body: Shannon Booth: hello again, I think https://github.com/whatwg/dom/pull/1479 should fix that NodeIterator issue. If you know of a less involved fix I'm all ears.

content.body: ha not sure why specfmt didn't catch any of these wrapping thing. I couldn't see them myself with the naked eye
It's really easy to find them when you look through the complete diff on GitHub and don't see the text lining up.
I guess I shouldn't say easy, but it's definitely possible.
yea I think I forgot to click "Refresh" in the diff view so I was seeing a previous version
(also maybe over-relying on specfmt which was so far rather dependable)
But then you should notice that as you read through it surely as it doesn't contain all changes? Your turnaround is quick, but I feel like I spend a lot of time on nits with your PRs.
That's fair feedback, I always try to improve.
Anyway, for this PR I've now triple-checked everything for nits and I think we're good

Another realization: Authors generally want to use the native behavior when it exists.

Fundamentally, *that* is what "tramples all over our design flexibility", not polyfills.

Even without a polyfill, authors will use feature detection and use the native functionality if it exists, and fall back to their own code only if it doesn't.

This can be done in a userland wrapper, a build tool, or even add hoc per usage.

Polyfills are simply abstractions to make this pattern more ergonomic for consumers, whereas a userland wrapper introduces one more level of indirection.

IMO, by not working on primitives that make good polyfills possible we do not preserve our design flexibility, we just get a buggier Web…

This can be done in a userland wrapper, a build tool, or even add hoc per usage.

Polyfills are simply abstractions to make this pattern more ergonomic for consumers, whereas a userland wrapper introduces one more level of indirection.

IMO, by not working on primitives that make good polyfills possible we do not preserve our design flexibility, we just get a buggier Web…

content.body: Noam Rosenthal: for https://github.com/whatwg/dom/pull/1478#pullrequestreview-4617350032 what happens if you clone a document and then `document.open()` it? This will give different results depending on how implementations track this state.
content.formatted_body: Noam Rosenthal: for https://github.com/whatwg/dom/pull/1478#pullrequestreview-4617350032 what happens if you clone a document and then document.open() it? This will give different results depending on how implementations track this state.

content.body: Noam Rosenthal: you probably don't even have to clone it depending on when a new parser is allocated.
content.formatted_body: Noam Rosenthal: you probably don't even have to clone it depending on when a new parser is allocated.

content.body: Right so I think you change the document open steps so they always set this to true. Which means that an XHR document with `document.open()` called on it will now do something different. At least specification-wise. Right?
content.formatted_body: Right so I think you change the document open steps so they always set this to true. Which means that an XHR document with document.open() called on it will now do something different. At least specification-wise. Right?

content.body: Right! That edge scenario is untested and the PR changes the behavior. Though I'm not sure what the correct behavior is. I think `document.open()` always allowing (or not allowing) DSD regardless of where the document came from makes more sense(TM)  when you think of what this flag comes to protect against.
content.formatted_body: Right! That edge scenario is untested and the PR changes the behavior. Though I'm not sure what the correct behavior is. I think document.open() always allowing (or not allowing) DSD regardless of where the document came from makes more sense(TM) when you think of what this flag comes to protect against.
